name: Fresno Unified School District
districtFact: The largest school district in California's Central Valley and the fourth largest in the state. Founded in 1885, Fresno Unified has grown alongside the San Joaquin Valley's agricultural economy into one of California's most diverse urban school systems.

about.title: About the District
about.content: Fresno Unified School District is the fourth largest school district in California, serving approximately 70,000 students across 100 schools in the Central Valley.

about.title: Programs & Curriculum
about.content: The district offers Dual Language Immersion programs in Spanish and Hmong, Career and Technical Education pathways, and one of California's most established Agricultural Education programs — reflecting the San Joaquin Valley's identity as the most productive farming region in the United States.

planningTips: Set back-to-school reminders for mid-July — confirm enrollment paperwork, childcare, transportation, and supplies well before the mid-August first day.

Sign up for FUSD's parent notification system before the first day of school to receive same-day alerts for AQI changes, outdoor schedule adjustments, and emergency closures. If your child has asthma, ensure their rescue inhaler prescription is current before mid-August.
Update all school emergency contacts before November — Tule fog bus delays can appear with less than an hour's warning and are communicated through the district notification system.

Mark Lincoln's Birthday (~February 12) and Cesar Chavez Day (March 31) on your childcare calendar at the start of the school year — both are Fresno Unified no-school days not observed in most other states.
Add Buyback Days to your personal calendar in August when Fresno Unified publishes the final schedule — these student no-school days are scattered throughout the year and easy to miss.

Book spring break travel well before March. For Yosemite day visits, set a reminder to book exactly 28 days before your target date when the rolling reservation window opens. Check the Yosemite National Park website for current entry requirements before booking.
Contact Fresno Unified's school choice office by October if you're interested in Dual Language Immersion, CTE, or Agricultural Education programs — some applications close before December.

districtFaqs.q: What is a District Buyback Day in Fresno Unified?
districtFaqs.a: Buyback Days (also called Staff Development Days or Teacher Workdays) are days when teachers and staff report to school for professional development, training, or planning — but students do not attend. These are typically scattered throughout the school year and are listed in the official Fresno Unified calendar.

districtFaqs.q: Is Fresno Unified the same as Fresno State?
districtFaqs.a: No. Fresno Unified School District (FUSD) is the K-12 public school system serving the city of Fresno. California State University, Fresno (Fresno State) is a separate university with its own academic calendar. Fresno State's semester schedule does not affect Fresno Unified school dates.

districtFaqs.q: Why is Lincoln's Birthday a holiday in Fresno Unified?
districtFaqs.a: California state law designates Abraham Lincoln's Birthday (February 12) as a paid holiday for school employees. Not all California districts close schools on this date, but many Central Valley districts, including Fresno Unified, observe it. This holiday is specific to California and is not recognized in most other states.

livingHere.intro: Fresno sits at the geographic center of California's San Joaquin Valley, surrounded by the most productive agricultural land in the United States. For families relocating here, the city's rhythms are shaped less by coastal trends and more by the land, climate, and economy that define Central Valley life.

highlights.label: Extreme Summer Heat
highlights.detail: Fresno regularly records temperatures above 105°F from June through August — among the hottest of any major California city. Air conditioning is essential, and outdoor afternoon activities during back-to-school weeks in late July are genuinely hazardous for young children.

highlights.label: Tule Fog Season
highlights.detail: Dense Tule fog rolls through the San Joaquin Valley from November through February, frequently reducing visibility to near zero. School bus delays and closures are common during fog advisories — fog, not snow, is the weather event Fresno families plan around each winter.

highlights.label: Agricultural Employment Cycles
highlights.detail: The Fresno economy is deeply tied to grape, citrus, almond, and stone fruit harvests. Seasonal agricultural employment peaks in summer and fall, affecting work schedules and family availability in ways that do not apply to urban districts elsewhere in California.

highlights.label: Yosemite National Park Access
highlights.detail: Yosemite's South Entrance (Wawona/Mariposa Grove) is approximately 65 miles from Fresno — roughly a 90-minute drive. Summer reservations for Yosemite Valley day visits sell out within minutes of release, often in January or February. Plan school-year break trips to Yosemite during spring or fall for better access.

highlights.label: Fresno Yosemite International Airport (FAT)
highlights.detail: FAT is a small regional airport with limited direct routes — mostly to West Coast hubs, Phoenix, and Las Vegas. Nonstop options are significantly fewer than at LAX or SFO. Most long-distance travel requires a connecting flight or a 3-hour drive to the Bay Area.

highlights.label: Air Quality and Valley Fever
highlights.detail: Fresno County has among the worst air quality in the United States, driven by agricultural dust, vehicle emissions, and wildfire smoke each fall. On high-AQI days, Fresno Unified may move outdoor PE classes indoors and shorten recess — families should enable school notifications and check AirNow before morning drop-off during fire season. Valley Fever (a fungal lung infection from soil dust) is endemic to the San Joaquin Valley; families new to the region should discuss it with their pediatrician, especially for children who spend time outdoors.
